organization_information.referred_by: Referred by Sarah Kim at Midtown Community Center

organization_purpose.purpose_of_the_organization_description:
Riverbend Youth & Community Arts Initiative is a small, community-based nonprofit founded in 2014 in the Riverbend neighborhood, a historically under-resourced area on the east side of the city. Our core purpose is to provide accessible, high-quality arts education and creative community-building opportunities for children, teens, and their families who otherwise would not have access to music, visual arts, theater, and digital media programming.

We primarily serve young people ages 8–18 from low-income households, many of whom are first-generation immigrants or the children of immigrants. Roughly 78% of our participants identify as Black, Latino, or Asian American/Pacific Islander, and approximately 65% qualify for free or reduced-price school lunch. Many of our families work multiple jobs, and parents frequently tell us that they cannot afford private music lessons, art classes, or summer camps that are common in more affluent neighborhoods. Our programs are designed specifically to close that opportunity gap.

Our work centers around three main program areas:

1. After-School Arts Labs: During the school year, we run daily after-school sessions in our storefront studio and in partnership with two local public schools (Riverbend Middle and Eastside High). Youth can enroll in classes such as beginner and intermediate guitar, digital music production, mural painting, ceramics, photography, and spoken-word/poetry. Classes are capped at 10–12 students to ensure individual attention. We provide all materials (instruments, art supplies, cameras, laptops, software) at no cost, and we arrange transportation stipends or bus passes for families that need them.

2. Summer Arts & Leadership Camp: Each summer we host a six-week day camp that combines arts instruction with leadership development and academic support. Mornings focus on skill-building in a chosen discipline (e.g., band, theater ensemble, visual arts studio, or film-making), and afternoons include workshops on topics like conflict resolution, goal-setting, public speaking, and college awareness. Teens who have been with us for multiple years can apply to be paid junior counselors, giving them their first job experience. Last summer, for example, one of our long-time participants, Jamal, co-led a hip-hop production workshop and then used those leadership skills to start a music club at his high school.

3. Community Arts & Family Engagement: We believe that the arts are a powerful way to build relationships and pride in the neighborhood. We organize quarterly community arts nights where youth showcase their work—band performances, short films, gallery-style art displays—and family members are invited for a free dinner and an evening of performances. We also support youth-led community projects, such as painting murals on previously vandalized walls or doing pop-up performances in local parks. One recent project involved a group of teens designing and painting a mural on the side of a local grocery store that now features portraits of neighborhood elders and quotes in English, Spanish, and Vietnamese.

Our organization benefits youth directly by giving them a safe, structured, and inspiring place to go after school and during the summer. Parents consistently report that their children are more confident, more communicative, and more motivated in school after participating in our programs. Teachers in our partner schools have noted improved attendance and engagement for students involved with Riverbend Arts. We also see tangible outcomes: last year, 92% of our high school seniors involved in our programs graduated on time, and 79% enrolled in some form of postsecondary education or training.

In addition to youth, our work benefits the broader community by activating public spaces with positive, youth-centered art and events. The community events we host routinely bring together neighbors who might not otherwise interact—long-time residents, newer immigrant families, and local business owners. These gatherings help reduce isolation, increase cross-cultural understanding, and create a sense of shared ownership over the neighborhood’s future.

We operate with a small core staff of four (an executive director, program director, part-time teaching artist coordinator, and an administrative assistant) and a network of about 15 paid teaching artists and volunteers. Many of our teaching artists grew up in Riverbend or similar neighborhoods and serve as relatable role models for our youth. Because funding is always tight, we are constantly balancing our desire to keep programs free or very low-cost with the need to pay staff and artists fairly and to maintain safe, well-equipped spaces.

In summary, Riverbend Youth & Community Arts Initiative exists to ensure that young people in our community—particularly those from families with limited financial means—have access to the transformative power of the arts. We do this by providing consistent, high-quality programming, removing financial and logistical barriers (cost, transportation, supplies), and engaging families and neighbors so that creativity becomes a shared, community-wide asset rather than a privilege reserved for a few.

support_requested.other_support_needed:
In addition to financial and volunteer support, we are also seeking in-kind donations and professional guidance in a few specific areas:

1. In-kind donations of equipment and supplies: We are in urgent need of gently used laptops or desktop computers capable of running basic audio and video editing software, digital cameras for our photography program, and musical instruments (particularly keyboards, drum kits, and acoustic guitars). We currently have youth sharing equipment in pairs or small groups, which limits their practice time and can be discouraging for students who are eager to experiment independently. We also go through a high volume of consumable art materials—canvases, sketchbooks, acrylic paints, brushes, and clay—so connections to local art supply stores or corporate donors would be extremely valuable.

2. Professional development and mentorship: Our small staff would benefit from pro-bono or reduced-cost consulting in nonprofit financial management, impact evaluation, and strategic planning. As we approach our 10-year anniversary, we are trying to move from a "scrappy startup" mindset to a more sustainable, long-term organizational model. Specifically, we would appreciate support in refining our data collection systems (for tracking youth outcomes over multiple years), developing a realistic three-year financial sustainability plan, and strengthening our board of directors.

3. Marketing and communications support: We do not currently have a dedicated communications staff member, so our outreach is mostly word-of-mouth and sporadic social media posts. Help from volunteers or professionals in designing a simple but cohesive branding package (logo refresh, print materials templates) and in setting up a more strategic social media and email communication plan would significantly increase our ability to reach new families, potential donors, and partners.

4. Transportation partnerships: Many of our youth struggle to get to our studio space safely, especially during the darker winter months. While we use a portion of our budget to provide public transit passes, we are exploring partnerships with local transportation providers, rideshare companies, or community shuttle programs to improve safe, reliable transportation options after school and in the evenings.

We are very open to creative forms of support and collaboration, including introductions to other funders, shared-use space partnerships with arts organizations or schools, or opportunities for our youth to perform or exhibit their work at community events sponsored by the Big Wheel Community Foundation or its partners.
